title: Control plane anatomy
summary: Five core components run the cluster: kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, and (in cloud clusters) cloud-controller-manager. Know exactly which does what.
points: **`kube-apiserver`** ‚Äî the only component that talks to `etcd`. Every `kubectl` call, controller, and `kubelet` watch goes through it. It is the cluster's hub and the one piece that horizontally scales easily.

**`etcd`** ‚Äî distributed, strongly consistent key-value store using the Raft consensus algorithm. Holds the entire cluster state. Production clusters run an odd number of members (typically 3 or 5) to tolerate failures; lose quorum and the cluster goes read-only.

**`kube-scheduler`** ‚Äî watches for pods with `spec.nodeName` empty, then picks a node based on resource requests, taints/tolerations, affinity, topology spread, and pod priority. It only writes the binding; it never starts containers.

**`kube-controller-manager`** ‚Äî a single binary running many controllers (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Job, EndpointSlice, ServiceAccount, etc.). Each controller runs a reconcile loop driving actual state toward desired state.
**`cloud-controller-manager`** ‚Äî splits cloud-specific logic out of the core: provisioning `LoadBalancer` services, attaching disks, and updating Node objects when the underlying VM goes away.
**Control plane vs. nodes** ‚Äî `kubelet` and `kube-proxy` run on every worker node, not on the control plane. On managed services like EKS/GKE/AKS, the control plane is hidden from you entirely.
Modern Kubernetes uses the term **control plane**, not 'master' ‚Äî that label was deprecated in 1.20 and removed from defaults.
traps: `etcd` is the source of truth, not a cache. Restoring from an `etcd` snapshot restores the cluster.
Only `kube-apiserver` reads/writes `etcd`. Controllers and the scheduler never touch it directly.
The scheduler does not run pods ‚Äî it only assigns them to a node. The `kubelet` on that node is what actually starts containers.

title: Workload controllers: pick the right one
summary: `Deployment`, `StatefulSet`, `DaemonSet`, `Job`, `CronJob` ‚Äî each solves a different shape of workload. The exam trades on picking the right one for a given description.
points: **`Deployment`** ‚Äî stateless, interchangeable replicas managed via a `ReplicaSet`. Supports `RollingUpdate` (default) and `Recreate` strategies. Use for web apps, APIs, stateless workers.

**`StatefulSet`** ‚Äî stable network identity (`app-0`, `app-1`), ordered startup/teardown, and per-pod `PersistentVolumeClaim` via `volumeClaimTemplates`. Use for databases, Kafka, Zookeeper ‚Äî anything where pod identity matters.
**`DaemonSet`** ‚Äî exactly one pod per matching node, scheduled even on nodes the scheduler would normally skip. Use for log shippers (Fluent Bit), CNI agents, `node-exporter`, kube-proxy itself.
**`Job`** ‚Äî runs pods to successful completion with `completions` and `parallelism` knobs. Pods are not restarted on success; failed pods are retried up to `backoffLimit`.
**`CronJob`** ‚Äî creates `Job` objects on a cron schedule. Time zone defaults to the controller's UTC; use `spec.timeZone` (stable since 1.27) for explicit zones.
**`ReplicaSet`** ‚Äî the low-level controller that maintains replica count. You almost never create one directly; `Deployment` owns it for you.
traps: 'Runs on every node' is always a `DaemonSet` hint, never a `Deployment` with `replicas: <node count>`.
`StatefulSet` pods get predictable ordinal names; `Deployment` pods get a hash + random suffix. If a question shows `app-0`/`app-1`, it's a `StatefulSet`.
A `Job` runs once to completion ‚Äî it is not the same as a `CronJob` running once. `CronJob` schedules `Jobs` repeatedly.
Deleting a `StatefulSet` does NOT delete its PVCs by default ‚Äî that's intentional, to protect data.

title: Service types: ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer vs ExternalName
summary: Four `Service` types, each with different exposure. The exam loves to ask 'which type would you use for X?'.
points: **`ClusterIP`** (default) ‚Äî stable virtual IP reachable only inside the cluster, implemented by `kube-proxy` rules (iptables, IPVS, or nftables). Pod-to-pod and pod-to-service communication.
**`NodePort`** ‚Äî allocates a port from the `30000‚Äì32767` range on every node and forwards to the Service's `ClusterIP`. External clients hit `<any-node-IP>:<nodePort>`.
**`LoadBalancer`** ‚Äî superset of `NodePort`. The cloud-controller-manager (or MetalLB on bare metal) provisions an external L4 load balancer that targets the NodePort on each node.
**`ExternalName`** ‚Äî pure DNS `CNAME` to an external hostname. No proxy, no `ClusterIP`, no selector, no endpoints. CoreDNS just returns the alias.
**Headless Service** ‚Äî `clusterIP: None`. DNS returns the pod IPs directly instead of a virtual IP. Required for `StatefulSet` per-pod DNS (`app-0.svc.namespace`).
traps: `NodePort` is not 'node-level' ‚Äî it's just a port opened on every node that forwards to pods chosen by the selector.
`LoadBalancer` requires cloud integration. On bare metal it stays `<pending>` forever unless you install MetalLB, kube-vip, or similar.
`ExternalName` has no `ClusterIP`, no endpoints, and no selector ‚Äî it is purely a DNS construct.
`Service` is L4 (TCP/UDP/SCTP). For HTTP host/path routing you need `Ingress` or Gateway API on top.

title: Probes: liveness, readiness, startup
summary: Three probe types with different jobs. Mixing them up is a classic exam trap.
points: **Liveness probe** ‚Äî 'is the container still healthy?' On failure, the `kubelet` kills and restarts the container according to its `restartPolicy`.

**Readiness probe** ‚Äî 'is the container ready to serve traffic?' On failure, the pod is removed from the `EndpointSlice` of any matching `Service` (no restart). Useful for warm-up and temporary back-pressure.
**Startup probe** ‚Äî gates liveness and readiness during slow boots. While the startup probe runs, the other two are paused. Once it succeeds, control hands over.
**Probe handlers** ‚Äî `httpGet`, `tcpSocket`, `exec`, and `grpc` (stable since 1.27). Tune `initialDelaySeconds`, `periodSeconds`, `failureThreshold`, and `timeoutSeconds` per app.
A pod with no readiness probe is reported `Ready` the moment its container starts ‚Äî often too eager for apps that need to warm caches or open DB connections.
traps: Readiness failure does NOT restart the pod. It only removes the pod from Service endpoints.
Liveness probes kill containers ‚Äî set them conservatively. An aggressive liveness probe on a slow-starting JVM is a famous self-inflicted outage.
If a startup probe is defined, the liveness probe is disabled until startup succeeds. That is the whole point.
Probes run from the `kubelet` on the pod's node ‚Äî they hit the pod IP directly, not via a `Service`.

title: ConfigMaps and Secrets
summary: Both inject configuration into pods. `Secrets` are base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default ‚Äî encryption is an opt-in `kube-apiserver` feature.
points: Both can be consumed as environment variables (`env` / `envFrom`), as command-line args via `$(VAR)` substitution, or as files via a `volumeMount`.
Files mounted from a `ConfigMap`/`Secret` update on the node when the object changes (with kubelet sync delay, ~60s). Environment variables are set at pod start and never refresh.

**`Secret` is base64, not encryption.** Anyone with read access to the API can decode it. To protect data at rest, enable `EncryptionConfiguration` on `kube-apiserver` (AES-CBC, AES-GCM, or KMS provider).
Both have a 1 MiB size limit per object (etcd value-size constraint).
`Secret` types include `Opaque` (default), `kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson` (image pulls), `kubernetes.io/tls`, and `kubernetes.io/service-account-token`.
Use `immutable: true` on `ConfigMap`/`Secret` to prevent accidental edits and reduce kubelet watch load.
traps: '`Secrets` are encrypted' is wrong by default ‚Äî base64 is encoding, not encryption.
Env vars from `ConfigMap`/`Secret` do not hot-reload ‚Äî you must restart the pod (or use a tool like Reloader).
RBAC on `Secret` is what actually protects them. A user with `get secrets` can read every value.

title: Pod networking: every pod gets an IP
summary: Kubernetes networking model: a flat L3 network where every pod gets its own IP and any pod can reach any other pod without NAT.
points: Each pod gets a unique cluster-routable IP. Containers in the same pod share the network namespace and reach each other on `localhost`.
Pod-to-pod traffic crosses nodes without NAT ‚Äî this is a hard requirement of the Kubernetes networking model.
The **pause container** (a tiny process that just sleeps) holds the network namespace so app containers can crash and restart without losing the IP.
**CNI plugins** implement the actual networking. The `kubelet` calls the configured CNI on pod creation/deletion to plumb the network. Common plugins: Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave, AWS VPC CNI.
**Service IPs** are virtual ‚Äî they live in `kube-proxy` rules (iptables/IPVS/nftables), not on any interface. That's why you can't ping a `ClusterIP`.
**DNS** is provided by `CoreDNS` running as a `Deployment` in `kube-system`. Pods resolve `svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local`.
traps: Two containers in one pod cannot listen on the same port ‚Äî they share a single network namespace.
'Containers in a pod share an IP' is true; 'pods share an IP' is false.
You cannot ICMP ping a `Service` `ClusterIP` ‚Äî it has no listener, only `kube-proxy` rules.

title: The autoscaler trio: HPA vs VPA vs CA
summary: Three different autoscalers operating on three different axes. Don't confuse them.
points: **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** ‚Äî scales the replica count of a `Deployment`/`StatefulSet`/`ReplicaSet` based on CPU, memory, custom metrics, or external metrics. Built into the controller-manager.

**Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)** ‚Äî adjusts pod CPU/memory `requests` and `limits` based on observed usage. Modes: `Off` (recommend only), `Initial`, `Auto`. In `Auto` it evicts and recreates pods to apply changes.
**Cluster Autoscaler (CA)** ‚Äî adds nodes when pods are unschedulable due to resource pressure and removes nodes that have been underutilized for a configurable period.
**HPA needs metrics** ‚Äî install `metrics-server` for CPU/memory, or a custom adapter (Prometheus Adapter, KEDA) for custom metrics.
**Karpenter** is an alternative to Cluster Autoscaler on AWS that provisions right-sized nodes directly from EC2 rather than scaling node groups.
**KEDA** (event-driven autoscaling) extends HPA to scale on external signals (Kafka lag, queue depth, cron) and supports scale-to-zero.
traps: HPA and VPA both targeting the same resource (e.g., CPU) on the same workload conflict ‚Äî use VPA in recommendation mode or scale on a different metric.
Cluster Autoscaler does not scale based on node CPU/memory utilization. It scales based on pending pods that cannot be scheduled.
HPA never sets `requests`/`limits`. VPA does that. CA never touches pods ‚Äî it adds/removes nodes.
Without `requests` set on pods, HPA cannot compute CPU utilization correctly.

title: NetworkPolicy: resource vs enforcement
summary: `NetworkPolicy` is a Kubernetes API object, but the CNI plugin actually enforces it. If your CNI doesn't support it, the policy is silently ignored.
points: **Default behavior** ‚Äî pods with no `NetworkPolicy` selecting them allow all ingress and egress.
**Selecting a pod** ‚Äî once any policy with a matching `podSelector` exists for a direction (ingress or egress), that pod becomes 'isolated' for that direction and only the allowed traffic passes.
**Rules are additive** ‚Äî multiple policies are OR-ed together. There is no explicit `deny`; you express deny by omission.

**Enforcement varies by CNI** ‚Äî Calico, Cilium, Antrea, and Weave enforce `NetworkPolicy`. Stock Flannel does NOT ‚Äî apply a policy and it is silently ignored unless you pair Flannel with Calico (canal) or another enforcer.
**Scope** ‚Äî `NetworkPolicy` is namespace-scoped. For cluster-wide rules, use vendor CRDs like `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` or Calico's `GlobalNetworkPolicy`.
**L4 only by default** ‚Äî standard `NetworkPolicy` selects on namespace, pod labels, IP blocks, and ports. L7 (HTTP method, path) requires service-mesh or CNI extensions.
traps: Applying a `NetworkPolicy` with stock Flannel and expecting enforcement ‚Äî silent failure.
There is no explicit deny rule. Default-deny is achieved by an empty-rule policy that selects all pods.
DNS often breaks when locking down egress ‚Äî remember to allow UDP/TCP 53 to `kube-dns`/`CoreDNS`.

title: Storage: PV, PVC, StorageClass, CSI
summary: Storage abstraction in three layers. Admins provision `PV`s (or a `StorageClass`); users claim them with `PVC`s; CSI drivers do the real work.
points: **`PersistentVolume` (PV)** ‚Äî cluster-scoped resource representing actual storage (EBS volume, NFS share, Ceph RBD). Lifecycle independent of any pod.
**`PersistentVolumeClaim` (PVC)** ‚Äî namespaced request for storage. Bound to a `PV` matching size, access mode, and class. A pod references the `PVC`.

**`StorageClass`** ‚Äî blueprint for dynamic provisioning. A `PVC` referencing a class triggers the CSI driver to create a `PV` on demand. Mark one as default with `storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"`.
**Access modes** ‚Äî `ReadWriteOnce` (one node), `ReadOnlyMany`, `ReadWriteMany`, and `ReadWriteOncePod` (single pod, stable since 1.27).
**Reclaim policy** ‚Äî `Delete` (destroy the underlying volume when the PVC is deleted) or `Retain` (keep it for manual recovery).
**CSI** (Container Storage Interface) is the standard plugin model ‚Äî every modern storage backend ships as a CSI driver. The in-tree volume plugins are deprecated/removed.
**`volumeClaimTemplates`** in a `StatefulSet` creates one `PVC` per pod ordinal, giving each replica its own persistent disk.
traps: `ReadWriteOnce` means one node, not one pod. Multiple pods on the same node can share an RWO volume ‚Äî use `ReadWriteOncePod` to truly restrict to a single pod.
A `PVC` without a `StorageClass` and no default class binds only to pre-created PVs (static provisioning).
Deleting a `PVC` with reclaim policy `Delete` destroys the underlying cloud volume. Production PVs should usually be `Retain`.

title: Ingress and Gateway API
summary: `Ingress` is the original HTTP router for Kubernetes. Gateway API is the successor ‚Äî more expressive, role-oriented, and multi-protocol.
points: **`Ingress`** ‚Äî L7 routing rules (host/path ‚Üí `Service`). Just a config object: it does nothing without an Ingress controller (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Contour) installed and watching.
**`IngressClass`** ‚Äî selects which controller handles a given `Ingress`. Multiple controllers can coexist by class.

**Gateway API** ‚Äî newer CRD-based standard, GA for `Gateway`/`GatewayClass`/`HTTPRoute` since 1.29. Separates concerns by role: `GatewayClass` (infra provider), `Gateway` (cluster operator), `HTTPRoute`/`TCPRoute`/`GRPCRoute` (app dev).
Gateway API supports cross-namespace routing via `ReferenceGrant`, weighted traffic splitting natively, and protocols beyond HTTP.
Neither resource provisions a load balancer on its own ‚Äî the controller behind it does (e.g., NGINX controller exposes itself via a `LoadBalancer` Service).
**TLS termination** ‚Äî `Ingress` references a TLS `Secret` per host; Gateway API uses listeners on the `Gateway` object.
traps: An `Ingress` resource with no controller installed does nothing. Creating the YAML is not enough.
Don't mix up `Ingress` (L7 HTTP/HTTPS) with `Service` of type `LoadBalancer` (L4, any TCP/UDP).
Ingress is feature-frozen (no new features) ‚Äî non-trivial routing should use Gateway API or controller-specific annotations.
Gateway API is not part of core Kubernetes ‚Äî you install the CRDs and a conformant controller separately.

title: Cloud native principles: 12-factor and immutability
summary: The mental model behind the whole CNCF ecosystem: stateless, declarative, immutable, automatable.
points: **Immutable infrastructure** ‚Äî never mutate a running server; rebuild the image and replace the instance. Containers and `Deployment` rolling updates embody this directly.
**Declarative APIs** ‚Äî describe desired state in YAML/JSON; controllers reconcile actual state to match. Imperative `kubectl create` exists, but the GitOps world is declarative.
**Stateless apps** ‚Äî state lives in backing services (databases, object stores, caches), not on the compute node. Pods are cattle, not pets.
**12-factor highlights** ‚Äî config in env vars, treat logs as event streams to stdout/stderr, disposable processes (fast startup, graceful SIGTERM), dev/prod parity, single codebase per app.
**CNCF definition of cloud native** ‚Äî 'scalable applications in modern, dynamic environments' built with containers, service meshes, microservices, immutable infrastructure, and declarative APIs.
**Loose coupling and microservices** ‚Äî small services with explicit APIs, deployed independently, communicating over the network.
traps: 'Cloud native' does not mean 'in the cloud'. It means built using cloud-native patterns ‚Äî perfectly fine on-prem.
Containers alone are not cloud native. A pet container with manual `docker exec` patches is not cloud native.
Microservices are an option, not a requirement. A well-designed monolith can still be cloud native.

title: Service mesh: what it gives you
summary: A service mesh layers observability, mTLS, and L7 traffic control onto service-to-service calls ‚Äî usually via sidecar proxies.
points: **Common features** ‚Äî mTLS between services (zero-trust), retries with timeouts, circuit breaking, request-level metrics and traces, traffic splitting, header-based routing.

**Architecture** ‚Äî every mesh has exactly two component groups: the **service proxy** (a.k.a. *data plane* / *sidecar* ‚Äî Envoy or `linkerd2-proxy` next to each pod) and the **control plane** (configures the proxies, distributes certs). Exam phrasing: *'service proxy and control plane.'*
**Istio** ‚Äî feature-rich, Envoy-based, complex. Supports both sidecar mode and *Ambient mode* (sidecarless: per-node `ztunnel` for L4, optional waypoint proxies for L7).
**Linkerd** ‚Äî lightweight, opinionated, ultralight Rust proxy. Easier to operate; fewer knobs.
**Cilium Service Mesh** ‚Äî eBPF-based, can run sidecarless by leveraging the kernel; integrates with Cilium CNI and `NetworkPolicy`.
**Consul Connect** ‚Äî HashiCorp's mesh, multi-runtime (works beyond Kubernetes).
traps: A mesh does not replace Kubernetes Services or DNS ‚Äî it runs alongside them, intercepting traffic via iptables/eBPF redirection.
Sidecars add real overhead ‚Äî CPU, memory, latency. Sidecarless modes (Ambient, Cilium) trade architectural complexity for resource savings.
mTLS in a mesh is between proxies, not between application processes. Apps still see plaintext on `localhost`.

title: CNCF landscape: who does what
summary: The 'which project lives in which category' grid is where most KCNA candidates lose points. Memorize the categories and the graduated projects in each.
points: **Orchestration & scheduling** ‚Äî `Kubernetes` (the only one that matters for KCNA).
**Container runtime (CRI)** ‚Äî `containerd`, `CRI-O`. Docker is no longer a Kubernetes runtime: dockershim was removed in Kubernetes 1.24 (2022).
**Networking (CNI)** ‚Äî `Calico`, `Cilium` (graduated 2023), `Flannel`, `Weave Net`, `Multus`.
**Storage (CSI)** ‚Äî `Rook` (Ceph operator), `Longhorn`, plus countless vendor CSI drivers.
**Service mesh** ‚Äî `Istio` (graduated 2023), `Linkerd` (graduated 2021).
**Observability** ‚Äî `Prometheus` (metrics, graduated 2018), `Jaeger` (tracing), `Fluentd`/`Fluent Bit` (logs), `OpenTelemetry` (unified signal pipeline), `Cortex`/`Thanos` (long-term Prometheus).
**App delivery / GitOps** ‚Äî `Argo` (ArgoCD/Rollouts/Workflows, graduated 2022), `Flux` (graduated 2022). Packaging: `Helm` (graduated 2020).
**Service proxy** ‚Äî `Envoy` (graduated 2018).
**Registries & supply chain** ‚Äî `Harbor` (registry), `in-toto`, `Notary`, `TUF`, `SPIFFE`/`SPIRE` (workload identity).
**Maturity levels** ‚Äî *Sandbox* (early), *Incubating* (proven adoption), *Graduated* (mature, broad adoption).
traps: Prometheus is metrics ONLY. Not logs, not traces.
OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral signal pipeline (SDKs + Collector), not a backend ‚Äî it feeds Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo, and the like.
Helm is packaging, not GitOps. Argo CD and Flux are GitOps controllers that often consume Helm charts.
`Docker Engine` is not a Kubernetes runtime any more, but `containerd` (which Docker uses internally) absolutely is.

title: Observability: three pillars, three tools
summary: Metrics, logs, and traces. Know the canonical CNCF project for each, and how OpenTelemetry sits across all three.
points: **Metrics** ‚Äî aggregated numerical samples over time. `Prometheus` is the de facto standard: pull-based, scrapes `/metrics` endpoints in the text exposition format, queries with `PromQL`. Stores time-series in a local TSDB.

**Logs** ‚Äî discrete events with structured context. `Fluentd` and `Fluent Bit` collect and forward; `Loki` indexes by labels (Prometheus-like) rather than full text; `Elasticsearch`/`OpenSearch` index everything.

**Traces** ‚Äî request paths across services with parent/child spans. `Jaeger` and `Zipkin` are the long-standing backends; `Tempo` is Grafana's trace store. Sampling is essential ‚Äî full tracing is too expensive.

**OpenTelemetry (OTel)** ‚Äî unified, vendor-neutral instrumentation for all three signals. Replaces the older OpenTracing + OpenCensus projects. Ships SDKs per language and a `Collector` that receives, processes, and exports.
**Exemplars** link metrics to traces ‚Äî a histogram bucket can carry a trace ID so you can jump from 'p99 spiked' to the exact slow request.
**`metrics-server`** is for HPA/`kubectl top` only ‚Äî short-lived, in-memory, low cardinality. It is not Prometheus.
traps: Prometheus is not for logs. A classic exam trap.
Grafana is a visualization layer, not a storage backend. It reads from Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, Elasticsearch, etc.
`metrics-server` ‚âÝ Prometheus. The former feeds HPA; the latter is a full TSDB.
OpenTelemetry doesn't store anything by itself ‚Äî you still need a backend (Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo).

title: GitOps: pull vs push
summary: Git is the single source of truth for desired cluster state. An in-cluster controller continuously reconciles actual state to match the repo.
points: **Four GitOps principles** (OpenGitOps CNCF working group): **declarative**, **versioned and immutable**, **pulled automatically**, **continuously reconciled**.
**Pull model** ‚Äî agent inside the cluster watches the repo and applies changes (`Argo CD`, `Flux`). Safer because the cluster never needs an inbound credential exposed to CI.

**Push model** ‚Äî CI pipeline runs `kubectl apply` from outside. Simpler to set up, but CI must hold cluster credentials and there is no continuous drift detection. **This is plain CI/CD, not GitOps in the OpenGitOps sense.**
**Argo CD** ‚Äî UI-heavy, `Application` and `ApplicationSet` CRDs, strong RBAC and audit trail, sync waves and hooks. Multi-cluster via `ApplicationSet` generators.

**Flux** ‚Äî composed of small controllers (`source-controller`, `kustomize-controller`, `helm-controller`, `notification-controller`, `image-automation-controller`); lighter footprint, GitOps-Toolkit style.
**`Helm` and `Kustomize`** compose cleanly with both ‚Äî Argo CD and Flux can render charts/overlays before applying.
**Drift detection** ‚Äî both tools constantly compare cluster state to Git and either alert (`Compare to desired state`) or auto-revert manual changes (auto-sync mode).

**Image-tag automation** ‚Äî GitOps tools don't build images. Closing the loop: **Flux Image Update Automation** or **Argo CD Image Updater** watches the registry and auto-commits the new tag back to Git, which triggers the reconciliation. Or your CI commits the new tag in a PR.

**Secrets in Git** ‚Äî never commit plaintext. **Sealed Secrets** (Bitnami) encrypts so only one cluster can decrypt; **SOPS** (Mozilla) value-level encryption via KMS/age/GPG; **External Secrets Operator** pulls real secrets from Vault/cloud KMS at runtime.
traps: GitOps means Git is the source of truth **and** a controller reconciles it. 'We store our YAML in Git' alone is not GitOps ‚Äî the reconciliation loop is what defines it.
Jenkins or GitHub Actions running `kubectl apply` is push-based CI/CD, not GitOps in the OpenGitOps sense.
Argo CD and Flux are not CI tools ‚Äî they **don't build images**. Pair them with a CI system that updates Git with new image tags.
**Terraform** is declarative IaC but typically **push-based** ‚Äî not a GitOps tool by the OpenGitOps definition (no in-cluster reconciler). Same with plain `kubectl apply` in a pipeline.
*'Which tool monitors a git repo for changes?'* ‚Üí **Flux or Argo CD** (both are correct). Terraform and Tekton are not GitOps controllers.

title: Deployment strategies: rolling, blue-green, canary
summary: Three strategies for shipping a new version, with different blast radius, infra cost, and rollback speed.
points: **Rolling update** (Kubernetes `Deployment` default) ‚Äî replace pods gradually, controlled by `maxSurge` (extra pods allowed above desired) and `maxUnavailable` (how many can be down). No extra infrastructure required.
**Recreate** ‚Äî `strategy.type: Recreate` kills all old pods before starting new ones. Brief outage; required when old/new versions cannot coexist (e.g., schema lock).
**Blue-green** ‚Äî two full environments side by side. Cut over traffic at once (Service selector swap or LB switch). Instant rollback, but doubles the infrastructure during the window.
**Canary** ‚Äî route a small percentage of traffic to the new version, observe error/latency, then ramp. Needs a traffic-splitting layer (service mesh, Gateway API weights, or `Argo Rollouts`).
**Progressive delivery** ‚Äî automating canary/blue-green decisions based on metrics; tools include `Argo Rollouts` and `Flagger`.
**Feature flags** complement these strategies: ship code dark, then enable per-user/percent without redeploying.
traps: Rolling updates give progressive pod replacement but no traffic-weight control ‚Äî a pod is either in the Service or out, all-or-nothing per pod.
Canary needs an intelligent router; a `NodePort` or plain `ClusterIP` Service cannot do weighted splits ‚Äî it round-robins endpoints.
Blue-green doubles the cluster footprint during the cutover window. Budget for it.
Setting `maxUnavailable: 0` and `maxSurge: 0` together makes a `Deployment` undeployable ‚Äî at least one must be non-zero.

title: Container runtimes and the CRI
summary: The `kubelet` does not run containers itself ‚Äî it talks to a CRI runtime over a Unix socket. Knowing the dockershim story is exam-critical.
points: **Container Runtime Interface (CRI)** ‚Äî the gRPC contract between `kubelet` and the runtime. Lets Kubernetes support multiple runtimes without code changes.
**`containerd`** ‚Äî the most widely deployed runtime. Originally extracted from Docker; CRI support is built in via the `cri` plugin.
**`CRI-O`** ‚Äî a minimal runtime built specifically for Kubernetes by the OpenShift community. No daemon beyond what CRI needs.
**Low-level runtime** ‚Äî both `containerd` and `CRI-O` shell out to an OCI runtime (`runc` by default) to actually create the Linux namespaces and cgroups for each container.
**Sandboxed runtimes** ‚Äî `gVisor` (`runsc`) and `Kata Containers` (lightweight VMs) are drop-in OCI runtimes for stronger isolation.

**Dockershim removal** ‚Äî the in-tree shim that let `kubelet` talk to Docker Engine was removed in Kubernetes 1.24 (April 2022). Clusters using Docker Engine must switch to `containerd`, `CRI-O`, or use the cri-dockerd shim from Mirantis.
**Images are still OCI** ‚Äî containers built with `docker build` work fine on `containerd`/`CRI-O`. The image format is independent of the runtime.
traps: 'Docker is removed from Kubernetes' is half right. Dockershim is gone; `containerd` (the engine inside Docker) is alive and well as a runtime.
Docker images and OCI images are interchangeable. Switching runtime does not require rebuilding images.
`runc` is not a CRI runtime ‚Äî it's the OCI low-level runtime that the CRI runtime calls.

title: RBAC and ServiceAccounts
summary: Role-Based Access Control gates every API call. `ServiceAccount`s are how pods (and CI/CD) identify themselves to the API.
points: **`Role` / `ClusterRole`** ‚Äî define a set of allowed verbs (`get`, `list`, `create`, `delete`, etc.) on resources. `Role` is namespace-scoped; `ClusterRole` is cluster-wide.
**`RoleBinding` / `ClusterRoleBinding`** ‚Äî bind a Role/ClusterRole to a subject: a `User`, `Group`, or `ServiceAccount`.
**`ServiceAccount`** ‚Äî a namespaced identity for processes running in pods. Every namespace has a `default` SA, mounted into pods automatically unless `automountServiceAccountToken: false`.
**Token projection** ‚Äî modern Kubernetes (1.22+) uses bound, time-limited projected tokens via `TokenRequest`, not the legacy long-lived `Secret`-based tokens.
**Verbs are additive; deny is implicit** ‚Äî RBAC has no deny rule. If no binding allows the action, it is denied.
**Aggregated ClusterRoles** ‚Äî `ClusterRole`s with `aggregationRule` automatically merge in matching child roles, used heavily by operators to extend `view`/`edit`/`admin`.
traps: Pods authenticate to the API as their `ServiceAccount`, not as a human user. CI tools that `kubectl apply` typically use a kubeconfig tied to a SA token.
There is no deny rule in RBAC. The only way to remove access is to not grant it ‚Äî or use admission controllers (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) on top.
`cluster-admin` is not a magic `*` flag ‚Äî it's a `ClusterRole` like any other. You can audit it with `kubectl describe clusterrole cluster-admin`.

title: Scheduling: requests, limits, QoS
summary: Resource `requests` decide where a pod runs; `limits` cap what it can use. The combination yields a QoS class that drives eviction order.
points: **`requests`** ‚Äî the amount of CPU/memory the scheduler reserves on a node. Sum of requests on a node cannot exceed allocatable.
**`limits`** ‚Äî the cap. Memory over the limit triggers an OOMKill; CPU over the limit is throttled (CFS quota), never killed.

**QoS classes** (assigned automatically by the kubelet):
 - `Guaranteed`: every container has equal `requests` and `limits` for both CPU and memory.
 - `Burstable`: at least one container has a request, but not all match limits.
 - `BestEffort`: no `requests` or `limits` set.
**Eviction order under node pressure** ‚Äî `BestEffort` first, then `Burstable` exceeding requests, `Guaranteed` last.
**CPU is compressible, memory is not.** Throttle vs. kill is a frequent exam distinction.
**`LimitRange`** sets per-namespace defaults; **`ResourceQuota`** caps total namespace usage.
**Scheduling primitives** ‚Äî `nodeSelector`, `nodeAffinity`/`podAffinity`, `taints`/`tolerations`, `topologySpreadConstraints`, and `priorityClass`.
traps: Without `requests`, the scheduler treats CPU/memory as zero ‚Äî pods can land on overcommitted nodes and starve.
Memory limits cause OOMKills; CPU limits cause throttling. Different failure modes, different debugging.
Taints repel pods unless they tolerate; node affinity attracts pods. Don't conflate the two.
`QoS = Guaranteed` requires `requests == limits` for **both** CPU and memory on **every** container in the pod.

title: Helm and Kustomize
summary: Two different ways to manage Kubernetes manifests: Helm templates with values; Kustomize layers patches over a base. They solve overlapping problems with different tradeoffs.
points: **Helm** ‚Äî a package manager. A *chart* is a directory of templated YAML plus a `values.yaml`; rendering produces manifests. Releases are tracked server-side in a `Secret` per namespace.
**Helm templates** use Go template syntax. Powerful (loops, conditionals, functions) but harder to lint statically ‚Äî the templates only become valid YAML after rendering.
**Helm hooks** run jobs at lifecycle points (`pre-install`, `post-upgrade`). Useful for migrations.
**Kustomize** ‚Äî built into `kubectl` (`kubectl apply -k`). A *base* of plain YAML plus *overlays* of strategic-merge or JSON patches. No templates, no DSL.
**Kustomize features** ‚Äî `commonLabels`, `commonAnnotations`, `namePrefix`, `images:` (tag override), `configMapGenerator`, `secretGenerator`, `replicas`, and `patches` for surgical edits.
**They compose** ‚Äî Argo CD and Flux can render Helm charts and then post-process with Kustomize (or vice versa). It's not either/or.
traps: Helm v3 has no Tiller and no cluster-side daemon ‚Äî that was Helm v2. Anything mentioning Tiller is outdated.
Kustomize is part of `kubectl`, but the standalone `kustomize` CLI is often more current ‚Äî `kubectl`'s embedded version lags.
Helm chart values are merged by the chart's logic; you cannot patch arbitrary fields the chart doesn't expose. Kustomize can patch anything.
`helm template` renders locally without contacting the cluster ‚Äî useful for piping into other tools.

title: Cloud Controller Manager: the cloud bridge
summary: The component that lets the same Kubernetes binary run on any cloud. When a `Service: LoadBalancer` is stuck `Pending`, this is who's failing.
points: **Why it exists** ‚Äî cloud-specific code (provisioning load balancers, querying node metadata, setting up routes) was lifted out of `kube-controller-manager` so vanilla Kubernetes is cloud-agnostic. CCM is the cloud-specific replacement.

**Three sub-controllers**:
 - **Service controller** ‚Äî creates/updates/deletes the cloud's load balancer when a `Service: LoadBalancer` is created. Writes the external IP back to the Service's `status.loadBalancer.ingress`.
 - **Node controller** ‚Äî sets node addresses, region/zone labels, deletes nodes from K8s when they're deleted in the cloud.
 - **Route controller** ‚Äî programs routes in the cloud's network so Pods on different nodes can reach each other.

**Where it runs** ‚Äî usually as a Deployment in `kube-system`, often labelled `cloud-controller-manager`. Cloud providers ship their own implementation (`aws-cloud-controller-manager`, `gcp-cloud-controller-manager`, etc.).
**On bare metal** ‚Äî there's no cloud, so no CCM. To still get `Service: LoadBalancer`, install **MetalLB** (or kube-vip) ‚Äî they speak BGP/ARP to advertise external IPs.
**Integration with CSI/CNI** ‚Äî CCM doesn't handle storage or networking driver logic; CSI drivers and CNI plugins are separate. CCM just covers the cloud control-plane operations (LB, node, route).
traps: **`Service: LoadBalancer` external-IP stuck `<pending>`** is almost always the CCM's Service controller failing ‚Äî missing CCM, no IAM, hit cloud quota, or bare-metal cluster with no MetalLB.
Made-up component names that look right: *Load Balancer Manager*, *Cloud Architecture Manager*, *Cloud Load Balancer Manager*. None of these exist.
CCM is part of the **control plane**, not the worker nodes. Don't expect to find it on every node.
Removing the `node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized` taint is the CCM's job ‚Äî Pods can't schedule on a fresh node until it's been tainted-then-untainted by the CCM.

title: Split brain and consensus protocols
summary: When a distributed cluster partitions, both halves can think they're the only one alive ‚Äî and silently diverge. Consensus protocols are the defense.
points: **Split brain** ‚Äî a network partition leaves two sub-clusters that can't see each other. Each elects its own leader, accepts writes, and the system silently diverges. When the partition heals, you have data corruption.
**Consensus protocols** force every write to be agreed by a **majority quorum**. Only the side holding quorum can make progress; the minority side stalls until rejoined.
**Raft** ‚Äî the modern, easier-to-understand protocol. Used by **etcd** (the Kubernetes control-plane store), Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, MongoDB.
**Paxos** ‚Äî the academic ancestor; used inside Google Chubby and Spanner.
**ZAB** (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast) ‚Äî used by Apache ZooKeeper.

**Why etcd HA uses an odd number of nodes (3, 5, 7)** ‚Äî quorum needs strict majority. With 3 nodes quorum is 2 (tolerates 1 failure); with 5 it's 3 (tolerates 2). Adding a 4th node doesn't increase fault tolerance and makes ties more likely.
**Kubernetes layering** ‚Äî etcd does consensus for cluster state. Apps you run on Kubernetes must do their **own** consensus (StatefulSet only gives stable identity, not coordination).
traps: **Replication is not consensus.** Replicating data without a quorum-agreed write order is exactly how you get split-brain divergence.
*'Which Kubernetes feature guards against split brain?'* ‚Üí **Consensus protocols.** Not Replication Controllers, not Rolling updates, not StatefulSet.
StatefulSet gives stable network identity but the consensus algorithm runs **inside the application** (e.g., the etcd or ZooKeeper process). Kubernetes provides the substrate, not the algorithm.
Even-numbered etcd clusters are worse than the next-odd-number-down. Don't run 4-node etcd.

title: Service discovery: DNS, env vars, and the mesh
summary: Kubernetes ships two built-in mechanisms for Pods to find Services, plus an external-mesh alternative. DNS is the standard; env vars are a stale fallback.
points: **DNS (CoreDNS)** ‚Äî the standard. Pods are configured with the cluster DNS at `/etc/resolv.conf`. Resolving `<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local` returns the Service's `ClusterIP`.

**Environment variables** ‚Äî at Pod start, `kubelet` injects vars for **every Service that already exists**: `<SVC>_SERVICE_HOST`, `<SVC>_SERVICE_PORT`. Available even without DNS, but **stale** ‚Äî Services created after the Pod aren't added.

**Service mesh** (Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect) ‚Äî sidecar (or ambient) proxies intercept traffic and consult a control plane. Adds richer discovery: weighted routing, mTLS, identity-based auth, circuit breaking.

**DNS records CoreDNS produces**:
 - **ClusterIP Service** ‚Üí A/AAAA record to the Service's virtual IP.
 - **Headless Service** (`clusterIP: None`) ‚Üí A/AAAA records to **every backing Pod IP**.
 - **ExternalName Service** ‚Üí CNAME to the external host.
 - **Named ports** ‚Üí SRV record at `_<port>._<proto>.<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local`.
**Server-side discovery** ‚Äî what Kubernetes does by default. Client hits a stable Service ClusterIP; kube-proxy load-balances. Client knows nothing about backends.
**Client-side discovery** ‚Äî client queries a registry (Eureka, Consul) for a backend list and picks one itself. Common outside Kubernetes.
traps: Env-var-based discovery **only sees Services that existed at Pod start**. New Services created after will not appear in `env`.
**Headless Services** return Pod IPs directly in DNS ‚Äî there's no virtual IP and no kube-proxy load-balancing. Used by StatefulSets so each Pod can be addressed by name.
CoreDNS is the cluster DNS server (since Kubernetes 1.13). `kube-dns` is the older one ‚Äî pick CoreDNS unless the question is about pre-1.13 history.

DNS resolution depends on `ndots: 5` and the search path in `/etc/resolv.conf`. Short names like `web` get five suffixes tried before a single absolute lookup, which can be surprising in latency-sensitive paths.

title: Image supply chain security
summary: Trusting a container image means knowing where it came from, what's in it, and that it hasn't been tampered with. Four pillars: sign, bill of materials, scan, verify.
points: **Sign images with cosign** ‚Äî part of the **sigstore** project. Records signatures in the **Rekor** transparency log; uses **Fulcio** for short-lived certificates. Proof of provenance.

**Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** ‚Äî a list of every package and version inside an image. Formats: **CycloneDX** and **SPDX**. Tools: `syft`, `cosign attest`. Lets you answer 'are we affected by CVE-X?' in seconds.
**Scan for vulnerabilities** ‚Äî **Trivy** is the most common (Aqua Security). Alternatives: Grype (Anchore), Clair, Snyk. Scan in CI before push, and again at admission.
**Slim base images** ‚Äî fewer packages = fewer CVEs. **Distroless** (Google), **scratch**, Alpine. Pair with **multi-stage Docker builds** so build-time dependencies stay out of the runtime image.
**Verify at admission** ‚Äî admission controllers like **Kyverno**, **Gatekeeper (OPA)**, **Connaisseur**, or the **Sigstore Policy Controller** can reject unsigned/unscanned images before they run.
**Build hygiene** ‚Äî pin tags to digests (`@sha256:...`), set an explicit non-root `USER`, never bake secrets into layers.
traps: An image that passes `docker scan` once is not safe forever ‚Äî new CVEs against the same dependencies are published every week. **Re-scan at admission**, not just at build.
Image **signing proves provenance**, not absence of bugs. You still need scanners.
SBOMs are useful even if you don't sign ‚Äî they let you answer impact questions for an existing image without re-deriving its contents.
Distroless images have no shell. Debugging needs `kubectl debug` with an ephemeral container that *does* have a shell.

title: Runtime security: keeping a running container honest
summary: Build-time scanning catches known CVEs. Runtime security catches the unknown ‚Äî privilege escalations, suspicious syscalls, sensitive-file reads ‚Äî while the container is alive.
points: **Falco** ‚Äî the canonical runtime security tool (CNCF graduated). Watches Linux **syscalls** via eBPF or a kernel module and alerts on anomalies (shell spawn in container, sensitive-file read, privilege escalation, outbound connection to unexpected destination).

**seccomp** ‚Äî filters which syscalls a container can make. Profiles: **`RuntimeDefault`** (recommended; the runtime's curated allow-list), **`Localhost`** (custom file on the node), **`Unconfined`** (no filtering ‚Äî bad).

**Linux capabilities** ‚Äî fine-grained slices of root: `NET_BIND_SERVICE`, `NET_ADMIN`, `SYS_ADMIN`, etc. Best practice: **drop ALL** in `securityContext.capabilities` and **add only what's needed**.
**AppArmor / SELinux** ‚Äî Mandatory Access Control profiles enforced by the kernel. AppArmor is path-based (Debian/Ubuntu); SELinux is label/type-based (RHEL/Fedora).
**Pod-level controls** ‚Äî `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true`, `runAsUser`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` (use `emptyDir` for writable scratch), `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`.
**Pod Security Admission (PSA) profiles** layer all of these into one switch: `Privileged` / `Baseline` / `Restricted`.
traps: `Unconfined` seccomp is an active downgrade ‚Äî never use it in production.
Falco does not block ‚Äî it **alerts**. Pair it with a response system (Falco Sidekick, custom webhook) if you want to act on detections.
Capabilities and `privileged: true` are different. `privileged` grants almost everything; capabilities are how you grant slices.
`readOnlyRootFilesystem` breaks apps that write logs or PIDs to disk ‚Äî mount an `emptyDir` at the path they need.

title: Serverless on Kubernetes
summary: Knative makes HTTP services scale to zero; KEDA makes event-driven workloads scale from external signals. Together they bring FaaS-style elasticity to Kubernetes.
points: **Knative Serving** ‚Äî HTTP services that scale to zero, auto-scale on concurrent-request count, support traffic splitting between revisions. Uses its own **Knative Pod Autoscaler (KPA)** by default.
**Knative Eventing** ‚Äî pub/sub via **CloudEvents**. Building blocks: `Source` (where events come from), `Channel`/`Broker` (the bus), `Trigger` (filter + sink), `Subscription`.

**KEDA** (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) ‚Äî wraps HPA. Adds **scale-to-zero** plus scaling on external events: Kafka lag, RabbitMQ queue depth, SQS, Prometheus, cron schedules, custom external metrics.
**OpenFaaS** ‚Äî a FaaS framework on Kubernetes; bring a function, OpenFaaS handles the HTTP gateway, scaling, and metrics.

**FaaS vs PaaS vs CaaS**:
 - **CaaS** ‚Äî Containers-as-a-Service. You bring the container; the platform schedules. Vanilla Kubernetes.
 - **PaaS** ‚Äî you bring code; the platform builds + deploys + scales. Heroku-style.
 - **FaaS** ‚Äî you bring a function; the platform handles invocation + scaling. Lambda, Knative.

**When serverless fits** ‚Äî sporadic, event-driven, or bursty workloads. **When it doesn't** ‚Äî steady high-throughput services, long-running stateful workloads, latency-sensitive paths where cold starts matter.
traps: Knative Serving and Knative Eventing are **separate components**. You can install one without the other.
**KEDA wraps HPA**; it doesn't replace it. The triggers run in KEDA, but the actual scaling happens through HPA under the hood.
Cold starts are real ‚Äî a Pod scaled to zero needs to be created, scheduled, and warmed up before serving the first request.
Knative imposes its own routing layer (Activator, Queue Proxy) ‚Äî that's extra latency and extra components to operate.

title: Cloud-native roles and personas
summary: Cloud-native systems need a handful of distinct roles working together. Knowing who owns what is exam material.
points: **Cluster admin / Platform engineer** ‚Äî owns cluster lifecycle, upgrades, RBAC, networking, ingress, observability stack, and the 'golden paths' developers self-serve onto. Cares about reliability, multi-tenant fairness, and cost.
**Application developer** ‚Äî owns app code, the container image, manifests / Helm chart for their service. Cares about time-to-deploy, debuggability, fast feedback.
**DevOps engineer** ‚Äî owns the CI/CD pipelines, IaC, automation between dev and prod. Cares about repeatability, deployment safety, lead time.
**SRE** (Site Reliability Engineer) ‚Äî owns SLOs, error budgets, incident response, runbooks. Cares about the reliability-vs-velocity trade-off.
**Security engineer** ‚Äî owns RBAC reviews, image-scanning policy, runtime security tools, audit log review, compliance. Cares about least privilege and supply-chain integrity.
**Network admin** ‚Äî owns CNI, ingress, DNS, service mesh. Cares about latency, segmentation, traffic visibility.
**Storage admin** ‚Äî owns StorageClass design, PV provisioning, backup/DR. Cares about durability and throughput.
traps: Roles overlap in small teams ‚Äî one person may own platform + DevOps + security. The exam still asks about the canonical division.
**SRE and DevOps are not the same.** DevOps is about automation and culture between dev and ops. SRE is about treating reliability as engineering with SLOs and error budgets.
Cluster admin is **not** a developer role. Developers shouldn't need cluster-admin to deploy their apps ‚Äî that's the platform engineer's job to make safe self-service possible.
'FinOps engineer' is increasingly its own role ‚Äî owns cost visibility, optimization, and chargeback.

title: CNCF governance and Kubernetes deprecation policy
summary: How CNCF projects mature, how Kubernetes makes decisions, and how long an API stays alive after it's announced as going away.
points: **CNCF structure** ‚Äî hosted by the Linux Foundation. The **Technical Oversight Committee (TOC)** accepts/promotes projects; the **Governing Board** handles funding and business affairs.

**Project maturity gates** ‚Äî **Sandbox** (experimental, welcomed in) ‚Üí **Incubating** (real production use, ‚â•3 independent end users, healthy contributors) ‚Üí **Graduated** (mature governance, security audit, broad adoption).

**Graduated examples** ‚Äî Kubernetes, Prometheus, Helm, Envoy, Argo, Flux, etcd, containerd, OpenTelemetry, Cilium, Istio, Linkerd, CoreDNS, Fluentd, Falco, OPA, cert-manager, KubeVirt, Harbor, Vitess, Rook‚Ä¶

**Kubernetes governance** ‚Äî **SIGs** (Special Interest Groups: api-machinery, network, scheduling, node, storage, etc.) own technical areas; **Working Groups (WGs)** are cross-SIG and time-boxed; **KEPs** (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals) drive any non-trivial change.

**Release cadence** ‚Äî roughly **3 minor versions per year**. Each minor release is supported for ~14 months. There's **no formal LTS** in upstream Kubernetes ‚Äî managed services (EKS, GKE, AKS) extend support windows themselves.
**Conformance** ‚Äî the **CNCF Certified Kubernetes** program ‚Äî distros must pass a test suite to use the Kubernetes name.
traps: **Deprecation policy** ‚Äî a stable (GA) API element must be supported for **at least 12 months or 3 minor releases**, whichever is longer. Beta APIs: at least 9 months or 3 releases. Alpha: no guarantees ‚Äî they can change or vanish in any release.
Sandbox does **not** mean unstable in practice ‚Äî many sandbox projects are widely deployed. The label is about CNCF process maturity, not code quality.
There is no formal upstream LTS for Kubernetes. 'My distro supports v1.X for 3 years' is your distro's contract, not Kubernetes'.
Conformance testing covers behavior, not features ‚Äî a conformant cluster can still be opinionated (e.g., default StorageClass).

title: SLI, SLO, SLA, and the error budget
summary: Reliability engineering's three R-words, side by side. Confusing them in a meeting is the fast track to the wrong conversation.
points: **SLI (Service Level Indicator)** ‚Äî an *actual measurement* of some attribute. Example: 'fraction of requests faster than 200ms over the last hour' or 'fraction of requests with HTTP 2xx/3xx'.
**SLO (Service Level Objective)** ‚Äî an internal *target* for an SLI. Example: '99.9% of requests under 200ms over a rolling 30 days.' Drives engineering priorities.
**SLA (Service Level Agreement)** ‚Äî an external *contract* with consequences (refunds, breach clauses) if violated. Always set looser than the SLO so the SLO has headroom.
**Error budget** ‚Äî `1 - SLO`. The amount of unreliability you're allowed in the period. If your SLO is 99.9%, your error budget for a 30-day window is 0.1% √ó ~720h ‚âà **43 minutes** of downtime.
**Burn rate** ‚Äî how fast you're consuming the error budget. Multi-window burn-rate alerts (Google SRE workbook style) catch fast-burn outages early without alerting on slow drift.
**The error-budget policy** ‚Äî when the budget is exhausted, freeze risky changes; when it's healthy, you can take more delivery risk. Engineering levers driven by reliability data.
traps: An SLI is not a metric ‚Äî it's a **specific aggregate** of metrics over a window (a fraction, a count, a percentile). 'Latency' is a metric; 'fraction of requests under 200ms over 1 hour' is an SLI.
If your SLO equals your SLA, you have no engineering headroom. Always keep SLO > SLA.
100% is never the right SLO ‚Äî perfect reliability has infinite cost and zero room for change.
An error budget is **per service per window**, not a global pool.

title: RED, USE, and the Four Golden Signals
summary: Three checklists for choosing what to measure on a service. They overlap on purpose ‚Äî pick one and apply it consistently.
points:
**RED** (Tom Wilkie) ‚Äî for **request-driven services**:
 - **Rate** ‚Äî requests per second.
 - **Errors** ‚Äî number/percentage of failed requests.
 - **Duration** ‚Äî distribution of request latencies.

**USE** (Brendan Gregg) ‚Äî for **resources or queues**:
 - **Utilization** ‚Äî average % of time the resource is busy.
 - **Saturation** ‚Äî extra work the resource cannot service yet (queue depth, lock waits).
 - **Errors** ‚Äî error events from the resource.

**Four Golden Signals** (Google SRE) ‚Äî for any user-facing service:
 - **Latency** ‚Äî time to serve a request (split successes vs errors).
 - **Traffic** ‚Äî demand on the system (req/s).
 - **Errors** ‚Äî rate of failed requests.
 - **Saturation** ‚Äî how 'full' the service is.
**Use RED for services, USE for resources.** A web service with a queue uses both: RED on the HTTP layer, USE on the queue.
**Histograms are how you measure Duration / Latency** ‚Äî server-side bucketed quantiles aggregate across instances, unlike client-side Summary metrics.
**Cardinality matters** ‚Äî labels like `user_id` or `request_id` explode time-series counts. Keep label values bounded.
traps: RED is for *requests*; USE is for *resources*. Don't apply USE to an HTTP API or RED to a Kafka broker ‚Äî use the right method for the thing.
The Four Golden Signals overlap with RED ‚Äî Saturation is the unique one. Treat it as 'how close to a hard limit are we?'.
Counting only successful requests in your Latency SLI hides errors; counting only errors hides slow successes. Track both with a clear policy.
High-cardinality labels (request IDs, user IDs, full URLs) blow up Prometheus memory. Use **traces** for per-request data, not metrics.

title: Open Policy Agent (OPA) and policy as code
summary: OPA is a general-purpose policy engine. In Kubernetes it powers admission control via Gatekeeper. Policies are written in Rego ‚Äî testable, portable, and not Kubernetes-only.
points: **OPA** decouples policy decisions from application logic. Apps ask: *'is this action allowed?'* ‚Äî OPA evaluates against a Rego policy and returns yes/no plus reasons.
**Rego** ‚Äî OPA's declarative query language. Not Python, not YAML. Designed for expressing rules over JSON-shaped data.

**Gatekeeper** ‚Äî the Kubernetes integration. A *validating* admission controller that runs OPA policies against API requests. Common policies: require labels, require resource limits, ban `:latest` images, restrict registries, enforce naming conventions.
**Constraint Templates and Constraints** ‚Äî Gatekeeper's Kubernetes-native API: a `ConstraintTemplate` defines the rule (Rego), a `Constraint` instance applies it to specific resources.

**OPA outside Kubernetes** ‚Äî Terraform plan validation (Conftest), CI/CD policy gates, microservice authorization (often via the Envoy `ext_authz` filter), database-query policy, anywhere you want policy-as-code.
**Local testability** ‚Äî `opa test`, `opa eval`, and `conftest` run policies against fixtures **before publish**. The CI pipeline tests policies the same way it tests application code.
traps: *'OPA policies must be written in Python'* ‚Äî false. Rego.
*'OPA is Kubernetes-only'* ‚Äî false. It's a general-purpose policy engine.
*'Policies can only be tested when published'* ‚Äî false. `opa test` runs against fixtures locally.
**Kyverno** is the alternative ‚Äî Kubernetes-native YAML policies, no new language to learn. Trade-off: less expressive than Rego for non-K8s policy.

title: Kubernetes object basics: namespaces and required fields
summary: Two foundational facts a fresh cluster gives you: four built-in namespaces, and a fixed top-level shape for every object you write.
points:
**The four namespaces a fresh cluster always has**:
 - **`default`** ‚Äî where any object you create without a namespace lands. (Note: `default`, not `kube-default`.)
 - **`kube-system`** ‚Äî system components: kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, scheduler, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CNI DaemonSets.
 - **`kube-public`** ‚Äî readable by everyone, **including unauthenticated users**. Holds the `cluster-info` ConfigMap. Mostly empty.
 - **`kube-node-lease`** ‚Äî node `Lease` objects used for fast node heartbeats (since 1.13, GA 1.17). Lighter than the old 'update Node status every 10s' approach.

**Required top-level fields** in every K8s object:
 - **`apiVersion`** ‚Äî which API version to use (`v1`, `apps/v1`, `networking.k8s.io/v1`).
 - **`kind`** ‚Äî what type of object (`Pod`, `Deployment`, `Service`, ‚Ä¶).
 - **`metadata`** ‚Äî name, namespace, labels, annotations, owner references, UID.
 - **`spec`** ‚Äî the desired state (most objects). ConfigMap and Secret use `data` instead.
**`status`** is **not** something you write ‚Äî it's set and updated by the controller that owns the resource.
**Where namespace lives** ‚Äî inside `metadata`, *not* at the top level. Exam options that put `namespace` next to `apiVersion` or `kind` are the wrong shape.
**`data`** is **not** a generic top-level field ‚Äî it's specific to ConfigMap and Secret.
traps: *'What namespaces does Kubernetes start with?'* ‚Üí **`default`, `kube-system`, `kube-public`, `kube-node-lease`** (exactly four). Made-up distractors: `kube-default`, `system`, `kube-main`, `kube-primary`.
*'Which fields are required in any K8s object?'* ‚Üí **`apiVersion`, `kind`, `metadata`** (and `spec` for most). Watch for distractors that put `namespace` or `data` at the top level.
Some cluster-scoped objects (Nodes, ClusterRoles, PersistentVolumes, StorageClasses) **don't take a namespace** at all. They're not 'in default' ‚Äî they're cluster-scoped.
`kube-public` is readable by **unauthenticated** users ‚Äî never put secrets there.

title: CI/CD vs IaC vs GitOps: don't mash them together
summary: Three flavors of DevOps automation that operate on different objects. They overlap in spirit and tooling, which is why they get conflated.
points: **CI/CD** ‚Äî automation that builds, tests, and ships **application artifacts** (container images, binaries). Tools: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, **Tekton**, Argo Workflows.

**Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** ‚Äî declarative or imperative code that provisions and configures **infrastructure**: cloud accounts, VPCs, VMs, IAM, DNS, the Kubernetes cluster *itself*. Tools: **Terraform** / OpenTofu, Pulumi, AWS CloudFormation, Bicep, **Crossplane**, AWS CDK, Ansible (config mgmt).

**GitOps** ‚Äî a Kubernetes operating model. An in-cluster controller (Argo CD, Flux) **continuously reconciles** cluster state to match a Git repo. Operates on Kubernetes objects (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps), not on cloud resources directly.
**Where they overlap** ‚Äî all three use Git as source of truth. IaC is often **executed by** a CI/CD pipeline (`terraform apply` in GitHub Actions), but that doesn't make Terraform a CI/CD tool.

**Crossplane** blurs the line between IaC and GitOps ‚Äî it represents cloud resources (RDS, S3, IAM) as Kubernetes CRDs, so Argo CD can reconcile them like any other object. Useful when you want one control plane.
**The mental hierarchy** ‚Äî IaC builds the cluster; CI/CD builds the apps; GitOps reconciles the apps onto the cluster.
traps: *'Which is GitOps?'* ‚Üí **Argo CD or Flux.** Terraform is IaC, Tekton is CI, Jenkins running `kubectl apply` is push CI/CD. Only the in-cluster pull-reconciler model is GitOps in the OpenGitOps sense.
*'Which is IaC?'* ‚Üí **Terraform / OpenTofu / Pulumi / CloudFormation / CDK.** Argo CD and Flux are not IaC ‚Äî they don't provision cloud resources (unless paired with Crossplane).
**Tekton is not GitOps.** It's a Kubernetes-native CI pipeline framework: pipelines and tasks are CRDs, steps run as Pods. Tekton builds and tests; it doesn't reconcile cluster state from Git.
**Kubernetes itself is provisioned by IaC**, then apps run on it via CI/CD or GitOps. Don't conflate 'the cluster' with 'what's running in the cluster.'
